segments.s404-g97: بیامد is a past-tense verb ('came') placed at the start for a slightly elevated/poetic word order; the following prepositional phrase ز هر گنبدی ('from every dome') gives the origin. میگسار (the person doing the coming) appears at the end as the subject. ز is the poetic 'from', and هر + گنبدی quantifies 'every dome/pavilion'.
segments.s404-g98: به نزدیک is a locative phrase meaning 'to/near the vicinity of' (به + نزدیک); آن is the demonstrative 'that' pointing to the following noun phrase; نامور شهریار is 'renowned king/sovereign' (adjective + noun). The whole phrase is a prepositional/locative complement modifying the verb 'came'.
segments.s404-g99: می‌خسروانی is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning 'in a kingly/royal manner' (here it qualifies the action that follows). به جام بلور is the goal/locative: به ... جام بلور = 'into/into the crystal cup' (جام is the noun 'cup', بلور specifies its material/quality). The sequence places manner before the object phrase in elevated style.
segments.s404-g100: This clause describes the handing/serving action: می‌داد is the imperfective/past habitual form 'gave/was giving' (here simple past sense). رخشان is an adjective 'radiant' describing whatever is given; چو + هور is a simile 'like Hoor/splendor'. گسارنده in context functions as the agent/actor 'the one who pours/serves' (cup-bearer), but the provided sense list does not match that exact meaning, so the semantic label is from the verse rather than the supplied senses.
segments.s404-g101: همه quantifies چهره دوستان ('all the faces of the friends'). برفروخت is a poetic past tense meaning 'lit up/was kindled' (here 'faces shone'); the verb follows the noun phrase in a compressed, elevated word order typical of poetry.
segments.s404-g102: دل دشمنان را is a definite object phrase (را marks the definite object 'the hearts of the enemies'). به آتش is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/with fire'. بسوخت is a past tense transitive/causative form 'burned' (it scorched/burned the enemies' hearts). The subject is omitted and understood from context (the host/scene).
segments.s404-g103: This is an idiomatic, slightly inverted construction: پسر is the topical subject ('the son'); خورد is used in the sense 'befell/wasfall on' (i.e., 'the memory struck/affected him'), not literal 'to eat'. با شرم ('with shame') modifies how the memory affected him; یاد پدر is the thing that 'fell upon' him — 'the remembrance of the father.'
segments.s404-g104: This is a parallel clause: پدر ('the father') is the subject; همچنان نیز ('still likewise') stresses that the same feeling applies in the other direction; یاد پسر ('the recollection of the son') is the object/thing remembered. Word order is subject + adverbial emphasis + object, typical in narrative poetry.
segments.s404-g17: از introduces a partitive/part-of relation here: 'a thousand of ...'. افگندنیهای is a deverbal noun (from افکندن/افگاندن) in plural meaning 'those things to be thrown/spread' (i.e., spreadable/laid-out pieces). دیبا is the item noun 'brocade' modified by the preceding phrase; word order is partitive: 'a thousand of the brocades to be spread'.
segments.s404-g18: بفرمود is a past, honorific/epic third-person verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here expresses result/purpose 'so that / in order that'. برنهادند is past plural 'they raised/put up/loaded' (bar- + nahādan). بار is the direct object 'the load / cargo'. The clause thus reads: 'he ordered so that they lifted/loaded the cargo.'
segments.s404-g19: چو here means 'when / as'. سیصد شتر is the numeral + noun 'three hundred camels' (number before noun). جامه چینیان is a genitive/Noun-Noun grouping 'the garments of the Chinese' (چینیان = from China). The whole phrase functions as an adverbial/time/setting phrase: 'when three hundred camels bearing Chinese garments...'.
segments.s404-g20: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. The following nouns are a list of types of cloth: منسوج (woven fabric/textile), زربفت (gold-brocade), and پرنیان (silken robe). The conjunctions (و and the euphonic وز) join the items, so the phrase enumerates the cargo's materials: 'of woven cloth, gold-brocade, and silken robes.'
segments.s404-g21: عماری is a plural/collective noun here (from عماره) meaning dwellings/tents/buildings or a heap of such things; in this context it means many piled items. بسیچید is a past tense verb 'they gathered/collected (in abundance)'. و joins the next noun phrase. دیبا جلیل is 'majestic/splendid brocade' (دیبا modified by the adjective جلیل). The sequence describes what they assembled: many dwellings/tents and glorious brocades.
segments.s404-g22: کنیزک is the direct object 'a little maidservant'. ببردند is past plural 'they took / carried off'. چینی here denotes 'the Chinese' (people) and دو خیل is a quantifying phrase 'two ranks / two companies / two hosts' (خیل = crowd/host). Word order places object before verb (poetic flexibility) and finishes with the agents and their number: 'two ranks of Chinese carried off the little maid.'
segments.s404-g23: به رخ is an adverbial phrase 'in/with respect to the face' (به + noun). چون بهار is a simile 'like spring' describing the face. و links to the next attribute; به بالا چو سرو is another simile 'in height/upward like a cypress' (بالا here = lofty/high). These are parallel adjectival comparisons describing appearance.
segments.s404-g24: میانها is an adverbial/locative 'in the middle / amid her (features)' (میان with plural/collective sense). چو غرو is another simile 'like the sunset/twilight' (غرو). و links the clause to the final participial phrase: به رفتن (literally 'in going', gerundial construction) + تذرو ('she scatters') — here تذرو is a present/epic-form verb 'she scatters/disperses', so 'and when she walked she scattered (beauty)'.
segments.s404-g33: همه باره is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'all at once' or 'utterly'; شهر is the grammatical subject; زد is a past tense verb here meaning 'struck/laid waste' used with شهر as the affected subject (poetic idiom: an inanimate city as agent of devastation or indicating it was struck down); بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'onto the ground' marking the result/place. Word order is topic (adverb) + subject + verb + place.
segments.s404-g34: برآورد گرد is a verbal phrase where برآورد (here 'raised/caused') takes گرد as its object meaning 'dust' (an older inversion order is normal in poetry); از بر و بوم چین is a genitival/partitive phrase 'from the land/soil of China' (بر و بوم is a fixed pair meaning 'land, homeland'). The verb + noun order (برآورد گرد) corresponds to 'dust was raised/rose.'
segments.s404-g35: سه پور جوان is a noun phrase 'three young sons' (سه numeral + پور 'sons' + جوان 'young'); را is the definite object marker / topicalizer marking the addressees; سپهدار is the speaker ('the commander'); گفت is the verb 'said' (past). Word order is topic/object marked (سه پور جوان را) followed by the subject/title and the verb (سپهدار گفت) — typical classical word order.
segments.s404-g36: پراگنده باشید is a present/plural imperative ('be scattered' / 'disperse, you (plural)'); با introduces an accompaniment or means ('with'); گنج جفت literally 'treasure (gifts) paired'—here meaning 'treasures in pairs' or 'paired treasure' to be taken along. The clause is an imperative command: disperse + with the treasure.
segments.s404-g37: به راه is a locative phrase 'on/along the road'; ار is the conditional particle 'if'; کسی is the indefinite subject 'anyone/someone'; سر بپیچد is a verbal idiom 'turn his head' = 'turn aside/deviate' where بپیچد is a present-subjunctive/optative form used in conditional clauses; ز داد is 'from justice' (ز = from, داد = justice). The whole clause: 'If anyone turns aside from the path of justice...'.
segments.s404-g38: سرانشان 'their heads/chiefs' is the direct object; به خنجر is an instrumental phrase 'with the dagger'; ببرید is an imperative plural form meaning 'cut off' (here an order to execute), and شاد is an adverbial/adjectival comment 'gladly' or 'be glad'. The clause is an imperative continuation of the command beginning in prior speech: 'cut off their chiefs' heads with the dagger — be glad.'
segments.s404-g39: شما is the addressed plural 'you'; راه سوی بیابان is a directional noun phrase 'the road toward the desert' (سوی = 'toward'); برید here is an imperative 'take/Make (your) way' (classical use of برید = 'go, set out' or 'take (the road)'). The clause is a direct command: 'You, go toward the desert.'
segments.s404-g40: سنانها is the subject 'their spears' (plural); چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; خورشید تابان is the noun phrase 'the shining sun' (تابان an adjective 'shining'); برید is a past or stative verb here describing what the spears did — poetically 'flashed/cut' and thus 'shone' in the simile. Word order places simile after the verb for emphasis: 'the spears ... like the shining sun.'
segments.s404-g65: وزان (compound of و + ز) is a poetic connector meaning 'and/from that' or 'from that place/time' + جایگه (noun) 'place' — together 'from that place'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' placed before its object ایران. کشید is a past-tense 3rd-person singular/plural verb placed at the end; here it means 'drew/moved toward' (a common poetic use of کشیدن).
segments.s404-g66: همه (quantifier) modifies گنج 'treasure' to mean 'all the treasure'. سوی + دلیران puts the destination 'to the brave ones' before the final verb. کشید repeats in the past and functions as 'brought/moved' (verb at sentence end) referring back to the same agent.
segments.s404-g67: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase 'every/all the city(ies) of Iran' (quantifier + noun + genitival modifier). بیاراستند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they adorned/arranged', appearing at the end as typical SOV/poetic order.
segments.s404-g68: This clause lists pleasures sought: می‌و (here 'mi' paired with رود means wine/fruit or wine) and رود (in classical/poetic usage can mean a string instrument) joined by و 'and'. رامشگران is the plural noun 'those who soothe/entertain' (minstrels/entertainers). خواستند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they desired/wanted', placed at the end and taking the preceding nouns as its objects.
segments.s404-g69: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (often written ز not از). دیوارها is the plural 'the walls'. جامه is the direct object 'garments'. آویختند is the past tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they hung' (verb-final), so the structure is 'From the walls they hung garments.'
segments.s404-g70: زبر here functions adverbially as 'on/above' or 'upon' (i.e. 'on top'), followed by the scents مشک (musk) و عنبر (amber/ambergris). همی is an archaic/poetic particle often used before verbs to indicate ongoing or emphatic aspect. بیختند is a past plural verb in form but its exact lexical sense (here 'scattered/poured/sprinkled') is not represented in the provided sense list, so the grammatical role is 'past action, they did X upon the musk and amber.'
segments.s404-g71: هوا 'the air/atmosphere' is the topic; پر (here 'full (of)') follows it to form 'the air full of ...'. ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from', and آوای رامشگران is a genitive phrase 'the sound/voice of the minstrels'. The clause is verbless (stative) and describes the scene: 'the air was full of the minstrels' voices.'
segments.s404-g72: Similarly, زمین 'the land' + پر 'full (of)' introduces the following nouns سواران 'riders/horsemen' and نیزه‌وران 'spearmen' in apposition; there is no separate finite verb—the line states the scene: 'the land was full of horsemen and spearmen.'
segments.s404-g25: This phrase functions as a prepositional/nominal phrase indicating accompaniment: (ابا) + خواهران یل اسفندیار = 'with the sisters of the hero Esfandiyar'. In classical/poetic spelling the initial form ابا here functions like the common preposition با ('with') or an archaic variant; خواهران is the plural noun 'sisters' followed by the epithet یل اسفندیار (the hero Esfandiyar) in apposition.
segments.s404-g26: برفتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they went/departed'. بت‌روی is a compound adjective: بت (idol/beautiful one) + روی (face) meaning 'idol-faced' or 'idol-like-faced' describing the following noun phrase; صد نامدار is a numeral + adjective phrase 'a hundred renowned (ones)'. Word order is typical Persian: verb after the noun phrase in narrative past, and modifiers (بت‌روی, نامدار) follow the nouns they qualify.
segments.s404-g27: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the source or group ('from the veiled-faced ones'). پوشیده is an adjectival past participle 'covered/veiled' modifying the following noun; رویان here functions as the group being described ('the veiled-faced'), and ارجاسپ is the agent (proper name) who performs the action later in the clause. The phrase orders source/adjective + noun + agent before the verb, as is common in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s404-g28: ببردند is a past 3rd-person plural verb 'they took' (here referring to Arjasp and his men or simply 'he took' in epic plural use). بامویه is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'with lamentation' (literally 'with wailing'), followed by a sequence of nouns joined by و: درد و رنج 'sorrow and suffering'. The structure lists the manner (بامویه) and the emotional effects (درد و رنج) after the verb.
segments.s404-g29: This is a listing: دو خواهر دو دختر یکی مادرش = 'two sisters, two daughters, one (is) their mother.' Classical Persian often omits the copula; یکی ('one') picks out a single individual and the enclitic ش (here attached to مادر) marks possession 'their mother'. Word order is noun phrases enumerated, with the possessor clitic attached to مادر.
segments.s404-g30: پر از درد is the adjectival phrase 'full of sorrow' (پر + از + noun). با سوک 'with mourning' adds circumstance/manner, و خسته 'and weary' is a further predicate adjective describing the mother; برش here is a fused preposition + pronoun (بر + ش) meaning 'upon her' or 'for her' — an archaic clitic construction that attaches the 3rd-person possessive/pronominal element to a prepositional base. Overall, the segment gives the mother's emotional state.
segments.s404-g31: چو is a temporal/subordinating particle 'when/as', introducing the circumstance. آتش is the subject 'fire'. به + رویین دژ is a directional phrase 'into the iron fortress' (به = into/towards, رویین = iron-clad, دژ = fortress). اندر is another locative preposition 'in/within' used in classical style, and فگند is an archaic past-tense verb 'cast/threw' (3rd-person singular or plural depending on context). The clause describes a past action setting the scene for the following result clause.
segments.s404-g32: زبانه is a poetic noun 'tongue' used metaphorically for 'a tongue of flame'; برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. به چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to the high sky' (به = to/towards, چرخ = sky/heaven, بلند = high). The result clause follows the temporal clause introduced by چو, showing consequence: flame rose up into the high heavens.
segments.s404-g57: سطرِ nominal phrase: 'سه فرزند پرمایه را چشم‌داشت' = 'he had expectation/hope for three valuable/able sons'. Word order is noun phrase + object marker را + noun چشم‌داشت (expectation). پرمایه is an adjectival epithet modifying فرزند and را marks the whole phrase as the object of داشتن in the next segment.
segments.s404-g58: این جمله وجهِ سببی/علمی دارد: ز + noun phrase gives the cause ('because of their late coming'), followed by a locative phrase به دل ('in the heart') and the past-tense verb داشت ('he had'). دل here is the inner locus for the feeling, and داشت is simple past possession/state ('he bore/harbored').
segments.s404-g59: پارهٔ قیدی زمانی/مکانی: 'به نزد پدر' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/into the presence of the father' (به + نزد), followed by the temporal clause introduced by چو ('when') and the past verb بیامد (came). Note the poetic order: verb بیامد precedes its subject پسر.
segments.s404-g60: جملهٔ اصلیِ خبری با فعل گذشتهٔ بخندید ('he smiled/laughed') که مضاف‌الیه/قیدیِ حال یا نحوۀ رفتار را با عبارتِ پیش‌رو مشخص می‌کند: با + هر یکی = 'with each one' و تاجور پس از آن به‌صورت اسمِ حال یا قیدِ حالت می‌آید ('like a merchant'). ساختارِ کوتاه و فشردهٔ شاعرانه است؛ فاعلِ بخندید برگرفته از عبارت پیشین است.
segments.s404-g61: که here begins a relative/explanatory clause attached to the preceding noun: 'که راهی درشت' = 'who was a coarse/rough traveller'. این‌که introduces reported content ('that / namely'), and من کوفتم is a first-person past statement ('I struck/beat/knocked') presented as the speaker's words. Note the poetic ellipsis: copula is implied after راهی درشت.
segments.s404-g62: عبارتِ علّیِ ساده: ز + دیر آمدنتان gives the cause ('because of your late arrival'), followed by the past 1st-person verb برآشوفتم ('I was upset/disturbed'). این جمله بخشی از گفتمان نقل‌شده است (کلامِ همان راهی).
segments.s404-g63: فعلِ مرکبِ خبریِ شاعرانه: 'زمین بوسه دادند' = lit. 'they gave a kiss to the earth' (i.e. kissed the ground). Inversion is poetic: object (زمین بوسه) + verb (دادند) and the subject هر سه پسر follows the verb.
segments.s404-g64: که introduces the content of what they express: 'چون تو' = 'like you', and the second که + باشد forms a subjunctive/optative relative clause ('who could be / who would be'). به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' and پدر is the predicate noun 'father' — together: rhetorical question 'who in the world could be a father like you?'.
segments.s404-g9: هيون is the subject (a proper name or noun) followed by خواست, the simple past tense of خواستن meaning 'wanted/ordered'. از هر دری is a prepositional phrase 'from every shore/door', and ده‌هزار is the direct object (a numeric quantifier 'ten thousand') placed after the preposition phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s404-g10: پراگنده is an adjective/participle 'scattered' that qualifies the implied troops or things; از دشت is the prepositional complement 'from the plain', وز کوهسار joins with poetic conjunctional nuance (وز = و + ز) meaning 'and from' followed by کوهسار 'mountains/highlands'. The segment is a verbless adjectival phrase modifying the quantity in the previous clause.
segments.s404-g11: همه is a universal quantifier 'all'; گنج is the noun 'treasure' and ارجاسپ is the subject (proper name). در here means 'door/gate' (not the preposition) and باز is an adjective/participle 'open' so در باز کرد is a light-verb construction where کرد (past of کردن) takes the noun phrase 'در باز' as its complement: 'opened the door (of the treasure)'.
segments.s404-g12: به + کپان is a prepositional phrase indicating manner/measure ('by/with کپان', here 'in handfuls' or 'by handfuls'); درم is the object 'dirham(s)' and سختن آغاز کرد is a compound verbal phrase where آغاز کرد 'began' takes سختن (an adverbial/qualifying word here) to indicate the manner or intensity of the action. Grammatically, this segment continues the action of distributing money begun by the verb کرد in the previous segment.
segments.s404-g13: هزار is a numeral 'a thousand' modifying اشتر; here اشتر functions as a noun (classical Persian 'camel' or a proper name) and از گنج دینار شاه is a genitive construction 'from the treasure (dinars) of the king' — از introduces the source 'from the treasure', with دینار specifying the type of treasure and شاه marking possession.
segments.s404-g14: چو here functions as a comparative/likening particle 'as/like'. سیصد is the numeral 'three hundred' and ز (poetic variant of از) marks origin/partitive 'of/from', followed by a list of items joined by و: دیبا 'brocade', تخت 'throne/platform', و کلاه 'and hats'. The whole phrase is a simile comparing the previous quantity to these riches.
segments.s404-g15: صد is the numeral 'a hundred' governing a series introduced by از/ز meaning 'of/from' — 'a hundred of musk and of amber and of gems'. The coordinating و links the list items; this is a simple noun list quantified by the initial numeral.
segments.s404-g16: صد again is 'a hundred' followed by از 'of' + تاج 'crowns'. وز here functions as a poetic connector (equivalent to و ز = 'and of/from') introducing a modifying phrase: نامدار 'renowned' qualifies افسران; افسران here means 'helmets' (classical usage) so the phrase denotes 'a hundred crowns and the renowned helmets of the commanders.'
segments.s404-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. گشتاسپ (proper name) is the subject; بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. رامش گزید is a compact clause where رامش (here functioning as the person/name or the object chosen) is the direct object and گزید is the past-tense verb 'he chose' (گزیدن). The subject (Gushtasp) is understood for گزید as well.
segments.s404-g74: به آواز او is an adverbial phrase 'at/by his voice' (به used here in the sense 'by/at'). او refers back to رامش. جام می‌درکشید uses the imperfective (می‑ prefix) with درکشید to indicate a past continuous or habitual action ('was drawing/lifting the cup' or 'drew the cup to his mouth'), so the whole clause describes Gushtasp's reaction occurring as the voice was heard.
segments.s404-g75: ز لشکر بفرمود is literally 'he commanded/from the army' (ز = 'from/of'), where بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate clause here with the sense 'that/so that', and هرک بود is 'whoever was (there)' — هرک = 'whoever' and بود is past 'was'. Together they introduce the group to be summoned.
segments.s404-g76: ز کشور کسی کو بزرگی نمود is a relative clause specifying whom to summon: ز کشور = 'from the country/realm'. کسی کو is an archaic relative construction (کو functioning as a relative particle 'who/that'), followed by بزرگی نمود, where بزرگی is 'greatness' and نمود (past of نمودن/نمود) means 'showed' or 'displayed'. The overall meaning: 'anyone from the realm who had demonstrated greatness.'
segments.s404-g77: همه is the plural subject 'everyone/all'. با درفش و تبیره is an instrumental/adverbial prepositional phrase 'with banner and tabīre' (با = 'with'), placed after the subject as is common in Persian. شدند is the plural past of شدن, giving a sense of 'came to be' or 'appeared/assembled' — here 'they came forward' or 'assembled with banners.' Note تبیره is an archaic/poetic noun whose precise sense depends on context (a kind of pennon/ensign or ornament).
segments.s404-g78: بزرگان لشکر is a noun phrase 'the great ones of the army' (compound genitive), and پذیره شدند uses پذیره (a nominative/participial noun meaning 'reception/accepted') with شدند to form 'were received/accepted' (passive or resultative). The verb agrees with the plural subject بزرگان.
segments.s404-g79: پدر رفت is a straightforward subject + past verb 'the father went'. با نامور بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'with renowned/bememorable wise men' (با = 'with'); نامور modifies بخردان (an epithet before the plural noun), which is an archaic plural form meaning 'wise ones'. Word order here (adjective before noun) is a stylistic choice common in poetry.
segments.s404-g80: بزرگان فرزانه و موبدان is an appositive/listing of companions: بزرگان 'the great ones' followed by two modifiers or appositives فرزانه 'wise/sagacious' and موبدان 'the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)'. The sequence lists the ranks/roles of those who accompanied him.
segments.s404-g113: سرآمد is placed before the time adverb کنون in typical Persian poetic order and here means 'has come to an end' (noun/verb sense). کنون 'now' locates the action in the present. قصه هفتخوان is the noun phrase 'the tale/story of the Seven Trials' with قصه (story) governing the compound هفتخوان (the seven trials/labors).
segments.s404-g114: به نام is the fixed prepositional phrase 'in the name of' (به used here in the sense 'by/in'), followed by جهان داور 'Judge of the world' (داور as a divine/judicial title). این را is the demonstrative object 'this' plus the Persian object marker را, and بخوان is a 2nd-person imperative 'recite/read' addressing the singer/reciter. The clause therefore issues the command to recite the foregoing in that name.
segments.s404-g115: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. داد here is used as 'justice/judgment' (a noun) meaning what he established or dispensed, and بر + دستگاه gives 'over/for a system or order' — so بر نیک و بد دستگاه means 'an order/system concerning good and bad'. The phrase describes the office/activity of the referenced being.
segments.s404-g116: خداوند here functions as the divine/lordly title 'the Lord/God' governing the following genitive-like nouns. خورشید 'sun' and ماه 'moon' are coordinated by و, and تابنده is the adjective 'shining' modifying ماه. Together they form the appositive/title of the divine judge mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s404-g117: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. شاه پیروز is the noun phrase 'the victorious king' (شاه modified by the adjective/epithet پیروز). بپسندد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form 'approves/should approve' (classical Persian mood used in conditionals). این is the demonstrative 'this' (object/topic referred to).
segments.s404-g118: نهادیم is the 1st-person plural past verb 'we placed/set' (an older/formal past used in epic diction). بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. چرخ گردنده is a compound where چرخ (here metaphorical 'wheel' = the rotating wheel of fate) is modified by گردنده 'turning/rotating'. زین appears as a noun 'saddle/trappings' following the phrase and in epic usage can emphasize the image of placing something upon the wheel; overall the clause says 'we set this upon the turning wheel.'
segments.s404-g49: This is a verbless descriptive clause: two nominative nouns followed by adjectival predicates. هوا (subject) is followed by the predicate خوش‌گوار, and زمین (subject) by the compound adjective پرنگار (پر- + نگار = 'full of ornament'). The coordinating و links the two noun–adjective pairs.
segments.s404-g50: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past simple 'you said' (گفتی). The reported clause that follows uses the preposition به + month name (به تیر) with اندر آمد ('came in/into') — an archaic/orderly word order where the verb آمد follows اندر and the subject of that clause (بهار) appears after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s404-g51: وزان is a fused classical particle (و + ز آن) meaning 'and/from that', introducing a phrase 'from that place' (جایگه). خواسته is used as the noun 'desire/wish', and برگرفت is a simple past verb 'took up/raised' — together meaning 'he took up (or resumed) his desire from that place.' Word order: prepositional phrase → object → past verb.
segments.s404-g52: همی ماند is a habitual/progressive past construction: همی (still/continuous) + ماند (past of ماندن) = 'he remained/kept being'. از expresses cause ('because of / at / from') governing the following noun phrase کار اختر شگفت, where اختر ('star') modifies کار ('work/deed') and شگفت is a postpositive adjective 'wondrous/strange'.
segments.s404-g53: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیکی شهر ایران is a genitive construction 'the vicinity/nearness of the city of Iran' (نزدیکی + [شهرِ ایران]). رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived' and completes the temporal clause: 'when he reached the vicinity of the city of Iran.'
segments.s404-g54: به جای = 'to the place of' (به + جای). دلیران و شیران is a coordinated noun phrase 'the brave and the lions' (شیران used metaphorically for heroic men). The clause ends with رسید (past 'arrived'), repeating the verb for rhetorical emphasis.
segments.s404-g55: دو هفته is a temporal measure 'two weeks'. همی بود is a progressive/past 'it/they were' (همی marking continuity). با + یوز و باز lists companions or hunting animals 'with cheetah and (a) hawk/falcon' — یوز (cheetah) and باز (hawk/falcon) are literal hunting animals common in classical Persian hunting scenes.
segments.s404-g56: غمی بود is an existential construction 'there was sorrow/grief'. از introduces the cause/source ('from' or 'because of') and governs the phrase رنج راه دراز, where رنج ('suffering/hardship') is specified by راه دراز 'the long road' (راه = road, دراز = long).
segments.s404-g41: This is a prepositional + purpose phrase: 'سوی هفتخوان من' places the motion 'toward my Seven Halls' (سوی before the place), with the possessive 'من' placed after the noun in poetic order. 'به نخجیر شیر' uses به + noun to mark purpose ('for/to [the] hunt'), with نخجیر ('hunt/expedition') followed by شیر as its specification ('a hunt for a lion').
segments.s404-g42: بیابم is first-person (optative/subjunctive) form meaning roughly 'let me / I will (go/find)' here functioning as the speaker's action. 'شما ره' names the addressees/your way (شما = you; ره = way) and مپویید is a present negative 2nd-person plural imperative/ prohibitive 'do not seek/follow'. دیر ('late') at the end is a predicative/adverbial comment — 'you will be late' or 'it is late (to pursue)'.
segments.s404-g43: نخستین is an adverb/adverbial phrase 'first'. بگیرم is 1st-person singular (subjunctive/indicative depending on register) 'I take / let me take'. 'سر راه را' is the object: سر ('head, start') modifies راه ('road, way'), and را is the definite object marker attaching to the phrase.
segments.s404-g44: ببینم is 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'let me see' or 'I will see'. 'شما را' marks 'you' as the direct object (را). 'سر ماه را' is another object/temporal phrase: سر + ماه = 'the start/beginning of the month', with the object marker را following the whole phrase; word order places the temporal phrase after the object for emphasis.
segments.s404-g45: سوی هفتخوان is the directional phrase 'toward the Seven Halls' placed before the verb for poetic word order. آمد is the simple past 'came' and the proper name اسفندیار follows (poetry often places the subject after the verb).
segments.s404-g46: به نخجیر marks purpose/destination 'to/for the hunt' using به + noun. با لشکری نامدار is an instrumental/ accompanimental phrase 'with an army illustrious/renowned' where با = 'with', لشکری = 'troops/army', and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned'.
segments.s404-g47: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک functions adverbially 'near/approaching'. آن جای سرما is a noun phrase 'that place of cold' (آن = that; جای = place; سرما = cold/winter) and رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s404-g48: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. خواسته is a participial/adjectival form 'desired/summoned' (here 'those he had summoned' or 'those desired'), گرد 'around' indicates gathering, بر جای 'on/upon the place' gives the location, and دید is the past tense 'saw' with the whole clause meaning 'he saw all (the summoned) gathered about the place.'
segments.s404-g81: بیامد is a simple past, 3rd‑person singular 'came'. به پیش is a fixed prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of' (به + پیش). پسر تازه‌روی is a noun phrase where پسر 'son' is modified postpositively by تازه‌روی (a poetic adjective 'fresh‑faced' or 'newly sprung'); the whole phrase is the semantic object/location of the coming.
segments.s404-g82: همه precedes the noun and here means 'the whole/entire'. شهر ایران is a genitive construction 'the city of Iran' (poetic omission could imply 'the cities of Iran' but grammatically it's 'city of Iran'). پر از marks fullness: پر (full) + از (of), so پر از گفت و گوی = 'full of talk/conversation'; گفت و گو is a split compound ('talk and saying') meaning 'conversation'.
segments.s404-g83: چو is an archaic/poetic conjunction meaning 'when'. روی پدر (face of the father) is fronted as the object of دید 'saw' — Persian permits object/adjunct fronting; شاه جوان at the end is the subject 'the young king' who performs the seeing. دید is past 'saw'.
segments.s404-g84: دلش is دل (heart) + enclitic possessive ش 'his' = 'his heart'. گشت is past of شدن 'became'. شادان and روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives coordinated by و, so 'became joyful and cheerful/bright‑souled.' روشن‌روان is a poetic compound adjective.
segments.s404-g85: برانگیخت is past 'roused/raised' (3rd sg). از جای = 'from its place' (از + جای). شبرنگ را is the definite object (را marks the object): the poetically named item شبرنگ ('shabrang', here 'variegated/ornamental standard') is what was raised. Word order places the verb before its object for poetic effect.
segments.s404-g86: فروزنده is an adjectival/participial form 'illuminating' or 'kindling' placed before آتش to qualify it. آتش جنگ را 'the fire of war' with را again marking the definite object — here the phrase stands in apposition to شبرنگ را, i.e. 'the shabrang, the war‑kindling fire.' This double‑object/appositive structure is a poetic device.
segments.s404-g87: بیامد is 'he came' (intransitive). پدر را ... گرفت is a separate clause: پدر را is the definite object 'the father' (را marking), and گرفت is 'took/seized'. به بر is an idiomatic locative 'to (his) bosom/into his arms' (به + بر), so the sequence describes: 'He came, (and) took the father to his breast.'
segments.s404-g88: پدر ماند = 'the father remained' (ماند past 'remained'). از کار او uses از to indicate the cause/point of reference ('at his deed' / 'because of what he did'). در شگفت = 'in wonder' (در + شگفت), so the whole clause means 'the father remained in wonder at his action.'
segments.s404-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” The clause has a poetic/inverted order: برخواند (past 3rd sing. “read aloud”) precedes the subject اسفندیار, so the literal order is “when that letter he read aloud, Esfandiyar.” آن نامه is the direct object “that letter.”
segments.s404-g2: ببخشید here is a past tense verb meaning “bestowed/gave” (classical Ferdowsi usage often shows a prefixed ب-). دینار is the direct object “dinar(s).” و links the two coordinated verbs; برساخت is a past verb “built/upheld/ set up” used here idiomatically as “set about / put the matter in order,” and کار is its object “the work/affair.”
segments.s404-g3: جز از introduces an exception or limitation: “except/from …”; here the structure is negative existential چِیزی نماند (“nothing remained”), with the genitive phrase جز از گنج ارجاسپ specifying what was exhausted—i.e. no part of Arjasp’s treasure was left. نماند is past of ماندن “to remain / be left.”
segments.s404-g4: همه modifies گنج: “all the treasure.” خویشان او is a genitive phrase “his relatives” (خویشان + enclitic pronoun او). برفشاند is a past transitive verb from فشاندن “to scatter/dispense,” here meaning he distributed or scattered (i.e. gave out) their treasure.
segments.s404-g5: سپاهش is written as two pieces here (سپاه + ش) — سپاه “army/host” with the possessive suffix ش “his.” همه emphasizes totality (“all of them”). زو is an archaic/poetic contraction of از او “from/through him.” توانگر شدند is past (شدند) + adjective توانگر “wealthy,” meaning “became wealthy.”
segments.s404-g6: از اندازه is a prepositional phrase meaning “beyond measure / from measure” (here idiomatically: exceeding measure). کار is “deeds/work” and برتر شدند (برتر + شدند) means “they became superior / surpassed.” Together: they exceeded all measure in their deeds.
segments.s404-g7: شتر بود uses بود as an existential/past “there were camels” (شتر here used collectively). و connects the second noun اسپان (plural horses/cavalry). به + دشت and به + کوه indicate location “on/in the plain and on the mountain.” Word order is straightforward descriptive.
segments.s404-g8: به introduces a locative or directional phrase “at/on.” داغ here is a poetic/archaic term and in context functions as a topographical noun (mount/crest); the supplied sense list does not include that meaning, so it is mapped below as unknown. سپهدار is a title “commander”; توران specifies the land (“Turan”), and گروه is “group/troop.” The whole phrase indicates the Turanian commander’s troop at the (mountain) dâgh.
segments.s404-g105: بپرسید is a classical past-tense 3rd-person form meaning “he asked.” The verb comes first in this clause (verb–subject order), followed by the proper name گشتاسپ as the subject. از governs هفتخوان and here means “about/concerning” (i.e. asking about the Seven Trials).
segments.s404-g106: This clause uses a topical/emphatic order: پدر را (the father marked with را) is placed up front as the indirect object/target of speech; پسر گفت is ‘the son said’ (past tense). نامه بخوان is reported speech: بخوان is a 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive/imperative ‘read/recite,’ giving the content of what the son said. را marks the definite/emphatic object (here the addressee).
segments.s404-g107: سخنهای دیرینه is a noun phrase 'ancient words/tales' (سخنهای plural + adjective دیرینه). یاد آوریم is a first-person plural form of آوردن in an optative/hortative sense (آوریم here = 'let us bring / we recall'); یاد is the object/thing being brought back to mind.
segments.s404-g108: This clause continues the action: به گفتار لب = 'into the speech/utterance of the lips' (به + گفتار modifying لب), while را refers back to the recalled words (object). به داد آوریم uses آوردن again (آوریم, 1pl hortative/subjunctive) and داد here is used idiomatically with آوردن—'to give/bring (something) to (expression)' i.e. 'to give them voice.' The line uses poetic inversion and compressed phrasing: literally 'into the speech of the lips we bring (them) to giving.'
segments.s404-g109: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when.' فردا here means 'on the morrow / in the future.' به هشیاری is an adverbial phrase 'with awareness/consciousness.' آن is a demonstrative 'that' referring to the matter just mentioned. بشنوی is 2nd-person singular present-subjunctive (or jussive) 'you hear' used in the protasis of a conditional.
segments.s404-g110: به is the preposition 'to/toward.' پیروزی is the noun 'victory.' دادگر functions as 'the just one' (here identifying whose victory or the just victory). بگروی is a 2nd-person singular present-subjunctive/hortative form meaning 'turn to / incline toward / seek'—an instruction/advice in the apodosis of the conditional.
segments.s404-g111: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/left.' The clause that follows is a relative/free choice expression: هرکس که گشتند مست = 'whoever had become drunk.' گشتند is past tense 'they became/turned (into),' and مست is the predicate adjective 'drunk/intoxicated.' The verb placed before the relative clause is a poetic inversion.
segments.s404-g112: یکی is an indefinite/distributive 'one' or 'a certain (one)' used here as 'each (one).' ماه‌رخ is an epithet 'moon-faced' (beautiful person). دست ایشان به دست is an idiomatic, compressed phrase 'hand (of) them to hand' = 'hand in hand / holding by the hand' (دست...به دست indicates mutual hand‑holding). Word order is elliptical; the clause describes that each left with a moon‑faced companion, hand in hand.
segments.s404-g89: بسی is an intensifier meaning 'much/many' modifying the verb خواند. خواند is a past tense verb 'he uttered/said' (here sense ‘perform/utter’). بر + فر = 'upon his far' (فر = royal glory); آفرین is an exclamation of praise ('acclamation') directed at that glory.
segments.s404-g90: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. بی‌تو = 'without you'. مبادا is an optative/negative wish meaning 'may it not be' (used to forbid or wish that something not happen). زمان و زمین are the coordinated nouns 'time and earth' serving as the subject/thing wished not to exist without 'you'.
segments.s404-g91: وزانجا is a fused adverbial 'and from there'. به ایوان شاه = 'to the king's iwan' (به + place, ایوان modified by شاه as a possessive/genitive). آمدند is the past plural 'they came' with the subject omitted (understood from context).
segments.s404-g92: جهانی here is used substantively/adjectivally 'of the whole world' (people from the world). ورا = 'to him' (vora). نیکخواه is a noun 'well-wisher' describing those people. آمدند repeats the plural past 'they came' agreeing with those visitors.
segments.s404-g93: بیاراست is a past tense verb 'he adorned/arranged' (classical poetic form). گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). ایوان و تخت are the direct objects 'the iwan and the throne' that he adorned; the verb appears first here by poetic word order.
segments.s404-g94: دلش = 'his heart' (دل + possessive suffix ش). گشت is the past 'became' (i.e. 'was turned into'), so دلش گشت خرم = 'his heart became joyful'. بدان نیک‌بخت is a prepositional/oblique phrase 'for/at that fortunate one': ب here marks direction/relation ('to/at'), دان is an archaic/demonstrative element ('that') and نیک‌بخت = 'fortunate one' (a person).
segments.s404-g95: به ایوانها = 'into/to the iwans' (به marking direction + plural ایوانها). در نهادند is the verbal phrase 'they put/placed in' (در = in/into; نهادند = past plural 'placed/put'). خوان is the direct object 'the banquet'. Word order in poetry is flexible, but verb agreement and locative marker show the intended meaning.
segments.s404-g96: به سالار گفتا = 'he said to the chief' (به + indirect object, گفتا = said). مهان را بخوان is an imperative command 'call/bring the guests' where مهان را is the definite object (را marks it). Note: the supplied senses list for بخوان does not include 'summon/bring in', so that specific sense is indicated below as unknown (-1).

sentences.s404-s49: From every dome a cupbearer came near that famed sovereign.
sentences.s404-s50: In kingly fashion he put wine into a crystal cup; the cup-bearer gave it, radiant like splendor.
sentences.s404-s51: All the faces of friends were lit up; the hearts of enemies were burned with fire.
sentences.s404-s52: The son, ashamed, was struck by the memory of his father; the father likewise still remembered the son.
sentences.s404-s9: He ordered that a thousand brocades be spread out, and they loaded them as cargo.
sentences.s404-s10: When three hundred camels bearing Chinese garments arrived — woven cloth, gold-brocade, and silken robes.
sentences.s404-s11: They gathered many tents/buildings and splendid brocades; two ranks of Chinese carried off the little maid.
sentences.s404-s12: With a face like spring and a height like a cypress, (in her middle) like the sunset; and when she walked she scattered (beauty).
sentences.s404-s17: The whole city was struck to the ground, and dust rose from the soil of China.
sentences.s404-s18: The commander said to the three young sons: (you) disperse, taking the treasures in pairs.
sentences.s404-s19: If anyone turns aside from the road of justice, cut off their chiefs' heads with the dagger — rejoice.
sentences.s404-s20: You (go) take the road toward the desert; their spears shone like the shining sun.
sentences.s404-s33: From that spot he drew toward Iran; he moved all the treasure to the brave men.
sentences.s404-s34: They adorned every city of Iran; they desired wine, the lute, and entertainers.
sentences.s404-s35: They hung garments from the walls; on top they scattered musk and amber.
sentences.s404-s36: The air was full of the sounds of the minstrels; the land was full of horsemen and spearmen.
sentences.s404-s13: With (or: alongside) the sisters of the hero Esfandiyar they went, a hundred renowned idol-faced beauties.
sentences.s404-s14: From the veiled-faced (women) Arjasp took five, with lamentation and sorrow and suffering.
sentences.s404-s15: Two sisters, two daughters — one their mother — full of sorrow, with mourning and weary upon her.
sentences.s404-s16: When fire was cast into the iron fortress, a tongue (flame) rose to the high sky.
sentences.s404-s29: He had expected three worthy sons; because they came late he carried anger in his heart.
sentences.s404-s30: When the son came to his father, he smiled; with each one he behaved like a merchant.
sentences.s404-s31: —who was a rough wayfarer and said, 'I struck (it); because of your late coming I was troubled.'
sentences.s404-s32: All three sons kissed the earth, saying, 'Who in the world could be a father like you?'
sentences.s404-s5: Hyun ordered ten thousand from each shore, scattered from the plain and the mountains.
sentences.s404-s6: Arjasp opened all the treasure-chamber and began, handing out dirhams by handfuls.
sentences.s404-s7: A thousand Ashtar (camels/loads) from the treasure of the king, like three hundred of brocade and thrones and hats,
sentences.s404-s8: a hundred of musk and of amber and of gems, a hundred crowns and famed helmets of commanders.
sentences.s404-s37: When Gushtasp heard, he chose Ramash; at his voice he drew the cup to his lips.
sentences.s404-s38: He ordered from the army that whoever there was from the realm who had shown greatness be summoned.
sentences.s404-s39: All came forth with banners and tabīre; the army's great men were received.
sentences.s404-s40: The father went with renowned wise men — the great sages and the mobeds.
sentences.s404-s57: Now the tale of the Seven Trials has come to an end; recite this in the name of the Judge of the world.
sentences.s404-s58: Who established the order of justice for good and evil — the Lord of the sun and the shining moon.
sentences.s404-s59: If the victorious king approves this, we have set it upon the turning wheel.
sentences.s404-s25: The air was pleasant and the earth richly ornamented; you said that spring had come in the month of Tir.
sentences.s404-s26: From that place he took up his desire, and he remained amazed at the wondrous workings of the star.
sentences.s404-s27: When he came near the city of Iran, he reached the place of the brave and the lions.
sentences.s404-s28: They were two weeks with cheetah and hawk; there was sorrow from the hardship of the long road.
sentences.s404-s21: I am going toward the Seven Halls to hunt a lion; do not follow my path — you will be late.
sentences.s404-s22: First I will take the head of the road; let me see you at the beginning of the month.
sentences.s404-s23: Esfandiyar came toward the Seven Halls to the hunt, with a renowned army.
sentences.s404-s24: When he approached that cold place, he saw everyone he had summoned gathered about the spot.
sentences.s404-s41: He came into the presence of the fresh‑faced son; the whole city of Iran was full of conversation.
sentences.s404-s42: When the young king saw his father's face, his heart became joyful and light‑spirited.
sentences.s404-s43: He lifted from its place the variegated shabrang, the fire that kindles war.
sentences.s404-s44: He came and clasped the father to his breast; the father remained astonished at his deed.
sentences.s404-s1: When Esfandiyar read that letter, he gave dinars and set the work in motion.
sentences.s404-s2: Nothing remained of Arjasp’s treasure; he scattered all the treasure of his kinsmen.
sentences.s404-s3: All his troops became wealthy through him; they surpassed measure in their deeds.
sentences.s404-s4: There were camels and horses on the plain and on the mountain; the Turanian commander’s troop was at the mountain (crest).
sentences.s404-s53: Gushtasp asked about the Seven Trials; the son said to his father, "Read the letter."
sentences.s404-s54: We recall ancient words; we put them into the speech of the lips and give them voice.
sentences.s404-s55: When tomorrow you hear that with wakefulness, turn toward the victory of the just one.
sentences.s404-s56: They departed — each one who had become intoxicated; each holding a moon‑faced one by the hand (hand in hand).
sentences.s404-s45: He uttered many acclamations upon his far (royal glory), saying that without you may time and earth not be.
sentences.s404-s46: And from there they went to the king's iwan; people from the whole world, his well-wishers, came to him.
sentences.s404-s47: Gushtasp adorned the iwan and the throne, and his heart became joyful for that fortunate one.
sentences.s404-s48: They set the banquet in the iwans; he said to the chief, 'Bring in/call the guests.'
